**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/raw_graphics/N-42-XXXIV/10.jpg`

---

## Figure Type

This is a **composite geological map sheet** at 1:200,000 scale, comprising three elements on a single sheet:
1. A **regional geological map** (main figure, upper portion)
2. A **geological cross-section** ("Разрез по линии Ю-Я" — Section along line Yu-Ya) along the bottom
3. A **схема использованных материалов** (index map of source materials used) in the lower left

## Coverage and Subject

The map covers sheets **N–42–XXXIV** (with sub-sheets **N-42-139, M-42-140, M-42-128-Г**), located in **northern Kazakhstan** (Казахская ССР), produced by the **Central Kazakhstan Production Geological Survey** (Центрально-Казахстанское производственное геологическое объединение) under the **Ministry of Geology of the Kazakh SSR**. The inventory number visible on the stamp is **№ 36522** (Republican Geological Fund copy). The cross-section runs along a **north–south transect labeled Yu-Ya (Ю-Я)**, with elevation profile and subsurface geology extending to approximately **–1000 to –1500 metres** below surface. The source materials index shows adjacent sheets **N-42-XXXIV, N-42-XXXV, M-42-IV, M-42-V**.

## Key Content

### Geological Map (Main Figure)
The map displays a highly complex, multiply-deformed Paleozoic terrane typical of the Central Kazakhstan fold belt. Key features include:

- **Colour-coded lithological/stratigraphic units**: The dominant colours are:
  - **Pale yellow/cream**: Likely Devonian or Carboniferous sedimentary sequences (sandstones, siltstones)
  - **Pink/red patches**: Granitoid intrusions (likely Hercynian granites), distributed across the western and eastern margins
  - **Dark blue/navy elongated bodies**: Possibly basic or ultrabasic intrusions (gabbro, diabase dykes), or structurally controlled metamorphic belts — several visible as elongated NW–SE trending bodies in the central portion
  - **Green areas**: Possibly older metamorphic basement, chloritized volcanics, or Ordovician–Silurian sequences
  - **Orange/brown patches**: Possibly Devonian volcanics or altered zones
  - **Light blue sinuous lines**: River/drainage network

- **Structural features**: Prominent **concentric/circular contour patterns** in the south-central portion of the map suggesting a **structural dome or ring structure** — potentially an intrusive complex, salt dome, or erosional window. This is a highly notable feature. Multiple **fault lines** (shown as bold straight lines) cut across the map in NW–SE and NE–SW orientations, defining a conjugate fault system consistent with Hercynian deformation.

- **Strike-and-dip symbols** are distributed across the map indicating bedding attitudes.

- **Mineral occurrence symbols** (small squares, crosses, triangles) are scattered across the map — these likely denote known ore occurrences, sample points, or geophysical anomaly locations.

- The eastern portion (right side) shows a separate map fragment (inset or adjacent sheet continuation) with similar lithological diversity and additional **pink granitic bodies**.

### Cross-Section (Lower Portion — "Разрез по линии Ю-Я")
- **Vertical scale**: Surface elevation ranges approximately **+300 to –1500 metres**; the topographic profile shows a prominent **peak (~400–500 m elevation)** near the center, corresponding to a topographic high likely associated with the dome/intrusive structure seen on the map.
- **Subsurface geology**: Below surface, the cross-section shows color-coded geological units extending to approximately **–1000 to –1500 m**, with:
  - Pink bodies interpreted as **granitic intrusions** at depth
  - Layered/colored sedimentary sequences flanking the intrusion
  - Apparent **thrust or reverse fault geometries** indicated by the dipping contacts
- **Tick marks and labels** along the bottom reference geological formation codes and ages (partially legible at this resolution).
- **Elevation axis** on the left is labeled in metres (visible values: 500, 400, 300, 200, 100, 0, –100, –1000, –1500 approximately).

### Source Materials Index (Lower Left)
Shows the overlap and coverage of previously compiled geological maps for this area, referencing survey vintages including what appear to be **1950s–1960s era surveys** by multiple authors (Дмитровский Ю.В., etc.).
